article_meta.title: Once upon a time, school students would submit an essay, and teachers would mark it

reasoning: Mostly neutral paraphrase that attributes evaluative claims and avoids the article’s more rhetorical flourishes. Minor inheritance of wording (e.g., “interrogating students”) and a couple of subjective adjectives, but no systematic framing adoption.

custom_reasoning.spin: Uses one loaded verb (“interrogating”) and mildly celebratory phrasing (“inclusive and innovative”), but otherwise neutral.
custom_reasoning.unsubstantiated_claims: Claims are grounded in the article (ISA warning, uneven adoption, teacher concerns) and/or clearly attributed.
custom_reasoning.opinion_as_fact: Evaluations are attributed; minor un-attributed positives (“inclusive and innovative”) appear once.
custom_reasoning.sensationalism: Tone remains measured; no dramatic language beyond a single strong verb mirrored from the source.
custom_reasoning.mind_reading: No claims about intentions without attribution.
custom_reasoning.slant: Covers both innovative uses and concerns, plus equity debate; balanced emphasis.
custom_reasoning.bias_by_omission: Main perspectives included; omits some detail (e.g., specific state tools) but not a major side.
custom_reasoning.subjective_adjectives: Uses “inclusive” and “innovative” in own voice once.
custom_reasoning.negativity_bias: Balances benefits with risks; no undue emphasis on negatives.
custom_reasoning.elite_populist_bias: Presents institutional (ISA) and practitioner (teachers/schools) views without privileging one unduly.
custom_reasoning.framing_neutrality: More neutral than source’s rhetorical setup; limited inheritance of one term.
custom_reasoning.perspective_completeness: Summary reflects key perspectives, but sources_represented lists only the outlet, omitting distinct voices (ISA, teachers, school).
